rulings.Case Title: LISA BROWN VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Responses To Supplemental Special Interrogatories, Set Two, And Requests For Production Of Documents, Set Two
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 15, 2018, Line 2: DEFENDANT UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'S Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Responses To Supplemental Special Interrogatories, Set Two, And Requests For Production Of Documents, Set Two. Pro Tem Judge Sanford Kingsley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Per the moving party, the motion to compel further responses to Supplemental Interrogatories Nos. 29, 30, 31 is no longer at issue. (Reply p. 3:18-21.) The motion as to responses to Supplemental Interrogatories Nos. 32 and 33 is DENIED as the wording of the Supplemental Interrogatories is overbroadly worded and does not seek information probative of the issues raised in the case. Hearing Required on Motion to Compel Production of Documents. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to skmediate@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: THOMAS JOSEPH SASSANI VS. KLP ENTERPRISES, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Elon Boms' Demurrer To 3rd Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 15, 2018, Line 19, Defendant Elon Boms' Demurrer To 3rd Amended Complaint Defendant Elon Boms' demurrer to the twenty-second cause of action in the third amended complaint for violation of the Unfair Competition Law is sustained with twenty days leave to amend to give plaintiff Thomas Sassani an opportunity, if he can do so in good faith, to allege in a UCL claim that Mr. Boms obtained money or property from Zozi as a result of an unlawful activity that Mr. Boms committed against Zozi. While Mr. Boms is technically correct that Mr. Sassani needed to obtain and did not have permission to add Mr. Boms to the UCL claim, there is no doubt that, had such permission been sought, it would have been granted, and thus Mr. Boms' argument about lack of permission is not a basis to sustain his demurrer. Neither Mr. Barth nor Mr. Boms has identified, nor is the court aware of, any published California authority requiring a UCL plaintiff to be a consumer or a competitor of the UCL defendant in a case based on the unlawful prong of the UCL. Imposing this requirement is inconsistent with the broad scope and language of the UCL and seems at odds with the case law permitting UCL claims based on a wide array of unlawful activity that can be committed against someone other than a consumer or a competitor. Liberally construed, the twenty-second cause of action adequately alleges that Zozi suffered economic injury as a result of Mr. Boms' unlawful activity, including Mr. Boms' causing Zozi to enter into economically unfavorable transactions. However, even liberally construed, the twenty-second cause of action fails to allege any facts supporting either an injunction or restitution against Mr. Boms in favor of Zozi and it appears that it is not reasonably possible that Mr. Sassani could allege any facts supporting injunctive relief against Mr. Boms. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Boms is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: EASTWOOD DEVELOPMENT INCORPORATED VS. MCKEE ELECTRIC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION To Compel Attendance At Deposition
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 15, 2018, Line 3: PLAINTIFF EASTWOOD DEVELOPMENT INCCORPORATED'S Motion To Compel Attendance At Deposition. Pro Tem Judge Sanford Kingsley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: There is no opposition to the motion, however there is no showing in the moving papers whether counsel for the deponents served an objection to the Notice of Deposition. See CCP 2025.450 (a) "If, after service of a deposition notice, a party to the action ¿without having served a valid objection under Section 2025.410¿" If the moving party can establish at the hearing that no objection was served, the Court will grant the motion and the requested monetary sanctions. If an objection was served, counsel shall produce it at the hearing and the Court will consider the objection and the motion in light of it. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to skmediate@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: LESLIE LAPAYOWKER VS. AIRBNB,INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Carlos Del Olmo'S Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Reinstatement Of Default (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 15, 2018, Line 20, Defendant Carlos Del Olmo'S Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Reinstatement Of Default (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2) Moreover, the trial court's decision to deny a motion to vacate the default was upheld on appeal based on an abuse of discretion standard and nothing in Hearn precludes this court from exercising its discretion to vacate the default entered against Mr. Del Olmo. While California policy, the interests of justice, and very possibly due process support vacating the default entered against Mr. Del Olmo in the exercise of the court's discretion per CCP section 473(b), that subsection provides discretion for the court to do so "upon any terms as may be just." Justice in this case mandates that Mr. Del Olmo, not Ms. Lapayowker, bear the monetary burden of Mr. Del Olmo's now acknowledged falsehoods and other misconduct. To facilitate arriving at an accurate number to be paid by Mr. Del Olmo, no later than November 28, 2018 counsel for Ms. Lapayowker must send an accounting with supporting documentation of all monies claimed by Ms. Lapayowker consistent with this order. No later than December 5, 2018 the parties are required to meet and confer regarding the amount claimed by Ms. Lapayowker in an effort to reach agreement on a number. If the parties are able to reach agreement, counsel for Ms. Lapayowker should so inform the court by email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to counsel for Mr. Del Olmo. If the parties are unable to reach an agreement, no later than December 12, 2018 each side may file supplemental papers limited to the issue of the amount that Mr. Del Olmo should be required to pay Ms. Lapayowker. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Del Olmo is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: LESLIE LAPAYOWKER VS. AIRBNB,INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Carlos Del Olmo'S Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Reinstatement Of Default
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 15, 2018, Line 20, Defendant Carlos Del Olmo'S Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Reinstatement Of Default (part 1 of 2) Defendant Carlos Del Olmo's motion for reconsideration of the September 27, 2018 order reinstating the default entered against him on February 13, 2018 is continued to December 19, 2018 to give the parties an opportunity to meet and confer and, if necessary, file supplemental papers regarding the amount Mr. Del Olmo will be required to pay plaintiff Leslie Lapayowker as a condition of setting aside the September 27, 2018 order. The court finds that there are sufficient changes of circumstances since the September 27, 2018 order was issued to warrant reconsideration per CCP section 1008. Alternatively, just as the court did with regard to Ms. Lapayowker's motion for reconsideration of the March 27, 2018 order setting aside the default entered against Mr. Del Olmo, the court treats this motion as an invitation to reconsider per Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal.4th 1094 without regard to compliance with the requirements of CCP section 100. Whether per CCP section 1008 or the court's inherent authority, the court intends to vacate the September 27, 2018 order per CCP section 473(b) on the condition that Mr. Del Olmo pay Ms. Lapayowker no later than 60 days after the entry of an order vacating the September 27, 2018 order all fees and costs Ms. Lapayowker incurred for: a) serving Mr. Del Olmo with the summons and complaint, b) opposing Mr. Del Olmo's motion that led to the March 27, 2018 order, c) filing Ms. Lapayowker's motion that led to the September 27, 2018 order, and d) opposing this motion. Considering all the evidence submitted on the prior motions and this motion, Mr. Del Olmo has shown sufficient facts to warrant the vacating of the default entered against him based on surprise, mistake, inadvertence and excusable neglect. Only "very slight evidence" is required to support a motion to set aside a default when the motion is promptly filed (as was the February 22, 2018 motion filed by Mr. Del Olmo) and the plaintiff has no credible claim of legal prejudice if the motion is granted apart from fees and costs incurred relating to the default. (Shamblin v. Brattain (1988) 44 Cal.3d 474, 478). Setting aside the default comports with California's strong policy of deciding cases on the merits. Moreover, per the unique facts of this case, maintaining the default and allowing a potentially large default judgment including punitive damages to be entered against Mr. Del Olmo is arguably violative of his federal and California due process rights since it is undisputed that at all times from the filing of the February 13, 2018 request for entry of default Mr. Del Olmo (through counsel) has made clear to Ms. Lapayowker that it was his desire and intent to oppose this lawsuit on the merits and he had contacted attorneys to do so. Neither Hearn v. Howard (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 1193 nor any of the other authorities cited by Ms. Lapayowker (nor any authorities known to the court) preclude vacating the default entered against Mr. Del Olmo. Hearn is distinguishable in many important respects, not the least of which are that the appellant in that case was an attorney and the opposing counsel had recommended the filing of a motion to vacate the default which was ignored by appellant. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: CLARE SPRINGS VS. WELLS FRAGO INSURANCE SERVICES USA, INC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Cross-Complaint; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 15, 2018, Line 7, DEFENDANTS WELLS FARGO INSURANCE SERVICES USA, INC, DUSTIN KLASS, DONALD GRAY'S Motion For Leave To File Cross-Complaint; Defendants Wells Fargo Insurance Services USA, Inc., Dustin Klass, and Donald Gray's motion for leave to file a cross-complaint is granted and the trial is continued to June 17, 2019 with all discovery deadlines triggered off the new trial date. Defendants have shown good cause to file a cross-complaint and permitting them to do so comports with the strong California policy of deciding cases on their merits. While defendants arguably could have moved to file a cross-complaint at an earlier time, there is no basis in the record on this motion to support a finding of bad faith or gamesmanship by defendants nor has plaintiff shown that he or the Trust will be prejudiced by the granting of the motion and continuing the trial. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which is entitled "Order Continuing Trial and Granting Leave to File Cross-Complaint" that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: OMAR SHAFIE VS. MEGHAN MCGARRY DANGANAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash And Motion For Protective Order Concerning Defendants Deposition Subpoena To The Permanente Medical Group And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Defendants And Defense Counsel
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 15, 2018, Line 5: PLAINTIFF OMAR SHAFIE'S Amended Notice of Motion And Motion To Quash And Motion For Protective Order Concerning Defendants Deposition Subpoena To The Permanente Medical Group And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Defendants And Defense Counsel. Pro Tem Judge Sanford Kingsley, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing Required. Defendants' counsel should be prepared to advise the Court of the date on which the subpoena to Permanente Group was withdrawn and provide proof to the Court and Plaintiff's of its withdrawal. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to skmediate@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SAMSON LAI ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION For Judgment On The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 15, 2018, Line 11, DEFENDANT SAM ROMERO'S MOTION For Judgment On The Pleadings Defendant Sam Romero's motion for judgment on the pleadings on the sole cause of action for harassment based on sex and/or gender in violation of FEHA alleged against him in the complaint is denied. The court did not consider the complaint filed by Ms. Montes in federal court or the misdemeanor complaint filed in San Mateo Superior Court. Neither Miller v. Department of Corrections (2005) 36 Cal. 4th 446 nor Proksel v. Gattis (1996) 41 Cal. App. 4th 1626 requires an allegation of more than a single consensual relationship to support an actionable paramour harassment claim. (Negrete v. Meadowbrook Meat Co. (C.D. Cal. 2012) 2012 WL 254039, *7 ("Miller expressly addressed Proksel and noted that it did not announce a per se rule that a plaintiff could not state a claim 'based upon favoritism arising from a single affair in a small office.'")). Proksel, 41 Cal. App. 4th at 1630-31 took "pains" to emphasize that the plaintiff in that case would have had a viable claim based on a single consensual relationship had she presented additional facts beyond favoritism to the paramour that, among other things, showed the existence of a hostile work environment. Miller, 36 Cal. 4th at 466 held that "an employee may establish an actionable claim of sexual harassment under FEHA by demonstrating that widespread sexual favoritism was severe or pervasive enough to alter his or her working conditions and create a hostile work environment." The determination of whether there is actionable "widespread sexual favoritism" as distinguished from non-actionable "isolated favoritism" depends not on the extent or number of paramour relationships, but the "effect" of those relationships on the workplace. (Id. at 463-64, 471-72). Plaintiffs have alleged sufficient ultimate facts showing that the alleged hostile effects of the alleged Romero-Montes relationship on them and the Station 2 workplace was severe, pervasive, widespread and resulted in significantly differential treatment of Station 2 employees based on gender. Most notably, and an allegation that Mr. Romero fails to adequately address in his memoranda, plaintiffs allege based on sufficient ultimate facts that among the many negative effects of the Romero-Montero relationship was the endangerment of the safety of those who worked at Station 2 and the public because Ms. Montes was permitted to avoid necessary training and was given preferential assignments which otherwise would have been given to firefighters who had greater familiarity with the equipment involved in the assignments. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: BRENDA MOORE ET AL VS. MEGAN ROHRER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Megan Rohrer, Ted Kuster, Shelley Carroll, Laurel Rohrer, Amy Zhou, And Grace Evangelical Lutheran Church'S Amended Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 15, 2018, Line 21, Defendants Megan Rohrer, Ted Kuster, Shelley Carroll, Laurel Rohrer, Amy Zhou, And Grace Evangelical Lutheran Church'S Amended Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint Defendants' demurrer to all six causes of action in the first amended complaint is sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to the first, second, third, and fourth causes of action for breach of contract, conversion, injunctive relief, and accounting and overruled as to the fifth and sixth causes of action for violations of the Corporations Code and Church Constitution. Plaintiffs have not adequately alleged standing for their breach of contract and conversion claims and are given leave to allege their capacities that they assert these claims and the facts supporting their theory or theories of standing. Injunctive relief is not a separate claim, but may be sought as a remedy for other claims. Accounting is not available where "the plaintiff alleges the right to recover a sum certain or a sum that can be made certain by calculation . . . the purpose of the accounting is, in part, to discover what, if any, sums are owed to the plaintiff, and an accounting may be used as a discovery device." (Teselle v. McLoughlin (2009) 173 Cal.App.4th 156, 179-80). Paragraphs 27, 28, and 46 allege certain sums of money of which defendants allegedly took possession. Plaintiffs are given leave to amend to allege, if they can do so in good faith, that amounts owed to them can be ascertained through a claim for accounting. The fifth and sixth causes of action are sufficiently alleged. Unlike Capolungo v. Bondi (1986) 179 Cal.App.3d 346, plaintiffs need not satisfy pleading requirements applicable to a negligence per se cause of action. The parties' disputes do not involve ecclesiastical matters. By applying neutral principles of law, the court will not impermissibly interfere with religious concerns. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: LIN CHEN ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 15, 2018, Line 14, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendant City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to the single cause of action alleged by plaintiff Guo Xi Chen in the first amended complaint is overruled. Both parties refer to the City's claim rejection notice and the court takes judicial notice of it as a matter not reasonably subject to dispute. That notice does not include the mandatory warning language regarding seeking the advice of an attorney prescribed by Government Code 913(b). Per Chalmers v. County of Los Angeles (1985) 175 Cal.App.3d 461, 465, failure to include the required warning language extends the time for filing suit to two years. Chalmers is on-point authority that this court is required to follow in the absence of any case law stating that the required warning does not extend the time to file suit when the claimant is represented by counsel. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: SALLIE HUNTTING VS. ARTHUR SCAMPA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, November 15, 2018, Line 15, DEFENDANTS ARTHUR SCAMPA, FATANEH SCAMPA, MAGNOLIA PROPERTIES, INC.'S Motion To Strike Portions Of Plaintiffs Complaint Defendants Arthur Scampa, Magnolia Properties, and Fataneh Scampa's motion to strike count two of the second cause of action in the complaint filed by plaintiff Sallie Huntting is granted without leave to amend. Civil Code 846 is a recreational immunity statute that does not apply to this case and thus the limitation of the scope of the immunity in that statute also does not apply to this case. The form complaint used by plaintiff comports with this understanding of the statute. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests.
